summary: Jeff Atwood explores the science behind how users navigate the web, drawing on research that compares information-seeking behavior to animal foraging. He traces the concept from Marcia Bates' 1989 'berrypicking model' through Pirolli and Card's 'information foraging' theory, which explains that users hunt for information the same way animals hunt for food. The core insight is that users follow 'information scent' and will instantly abandon pages that don't signal relevance. Atwood argues this behavior stems from basic human laziness—people want maximum benefit for minimum effort. The practical implication is stark: users form impressions in milliseconds and abandon sites within two minutes if they don't find value.
key_points: Users don't execute single queries but engage in iterative 'berrypicking'—moving fluidly between search and browse, following citations, subjects, and authors
Information foraging theory draws direct parallels between animal hunting strategies and human information-seeking: wolves hunt like search engines, spiders trap like filters
Users follow 'information scent'—they must sense they're 'getting warmer' or they'll abandon the hunt immediately
The underlying principle is human laziness: people seek maximum benefit for minimum effort, making them ruthless evaluators of content
Better search engines actually decrease time spent on individual sites because users can more easily find alternatives
money_quotes: Users will click the back button nearly instantly when they don't catch a whiff of the right information from the current page.
In any case, people like to get maximum benefit for minimum effort.
People left the sites they found useless within less than two minutes.
You better hope the information scent is strong on your page, because Informavores' fingers are always hovering over the back button.
So if you've ever wondered why users behave like animals online, now you know.
key_insight: Users are informavores who hunt for content with the same ruthless efficiency as animals foraging for food—if your page doesn't immediately signal value, they're gone in seconds.
